front: What is the core statistical assumption of the Naive Bayes classifier in NLP?
back: The 'Naive' assumption is that the features (words in a document) are conditionally independent of each other given the class label. This means the presence or absence of a word is assumed to be unrelated to the presence or absence of any other word.

front: In the context of training a Naive Bayes model for text classification, what is the purpose of calculating the prior probability P(c)?
back: The prior probability P(c) is the probability of a class 'c' based on its frequency in the training dataset. It represents our initial belief about how likely a class is before observing any evidence (the words in the document). It is calculated as: P(c) = (Number of documents in class c) / (Total number of documents).

front: What is the Likelihood P(w|c) in Naive Bayes, and why is Laplace (add-one) smoothing applied to its calculation?
back: P(w|c) is the probability of a word 'w' appearing in a document, given that the document belongs to class 'c'. It is calculated as: Count(w, c) / (Total word count in class c). Laplace smoothing adds 1 to each word's count to avoid zero probabilities for words that appear in the test set but were never seen in the training set for a given class, which would otherwise make the entire probability product zero.

front: How is the joint probability P(document, c) calculated during the training phase of a Naive Bayes classifier?
back: Under the naive assumption, the joint probability of observing the set of words in a document and it belonging to class c is calculated as the product of the prior probability and all the individual word likelihoods: P(document, c) = P(c) * ∏(for each word w in document) P(w|c). In practice, log probabilities are used to avoid underflow from multiplying many small numbers.

front: What is the fundamental rule (Bayes' Theorem) used to make a prediction for a new document after the model is trained?
back: The classifier uses Bayes' Theorem to calculate the posterior probability: P(c|document) = [P(document|c) * P(c)] / P(document). Since P(document) is constant for all classes, the prediction is made by selecting the class c that maximizes the numerator: argmax_c [ P(c) * ∏ P(w_i|c) ].
